title: What is the Spring Equinox?
summary: Spring starts on Friday with the vernal equinox. This is a special day when the sun is directly over the equator, making day and night almost equal in length. It happens because the Earth is tilted, but during the equinox, it's not leaning toward or away from the sun. This means both the northern and southern parts of Earth get the same sunlight. People all over the world celebrate this day. For example, in Japan, it's a holiday, and in Mexico, they watch shadows make a snake shape on a pyramid. After the equinox, days get longer in the north and shorter in the south until summer.

keywords.explanation: a day when day and night are almost the same length
the star that gives Earth light and warmth
how Earth leans as it goes around the sun
to have fun or honor a special day
one half of Earth, like the north or south part

questions.question: What do people in Japan do on Vernal Equinox Day?
What happens to days in the Northern Hemisphere after the equinox?

background_read: The vernal equinox marks the start of spring in the Northern Hemisphere. It's a time when the sun is directly above the equator, making day and night almost the same length. This happens because Earth orbits the sun at an angle, but during the equinox, it's not tilted toward or away. People around the world celebrate this event with holidays and traditions, like in Japan and Mexico. After the equinox, days gradually get longer in the north as we move toward summer.
Article_Structure: WHO: People all over the world, like in Japan and Mexico
WHAT: The vernal equinox, a day when spring starts and day and night are equal
WHERE: On Earth, especially at the equator and in different countries
WHY: To mark the change of seasons and celebrate with traditions

perspectives.description: Explains how Earth's tilt and orbit cause the equinox with equal sunlight.
Describes how different cultures celebrate the equinox with holidays and events.
